original_abstract: Investigating the transport mechanisms of mercury ions (Hg(II)) in the human intestine has traditionally relied on animal models or static cell cultures, both of which fail to accurately replicate the complex physiological microenvironment or provide real-time monitoring of cellular responses. In this study, we developed a biomimetic gut-on-a-chip platform integrated with label-free sensors to simulate intestinal physiology and monitor Hg(II) absorption in situ. The device recreates the dynamic intestinal environment by applying precise fluid shear stress (0.02 dyne/cm²) and cyclic mechanical strain (1%, 0.15 Hz). Integrated transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) sensors allowed for the noninvasive tracking of tight junction formation and barrier integrity, while electrochemical sensors enabled the quantification of Hg(II) transport. Our results demonstrate that the combination of fluid flow and mechanical stretching promotes the formation of intestinal villi-like structures and enhances cellular differentiation compared to static models. Exposure experiments revealed that Hg(II) causes significant physical damage to the epithelial barrier, evidenced by a decrease in TEER and downregulation of ZO-1 and ezrin proteins. Furthermore, we observed that increasing mechanical tensile strain from 1% to 5% led to a 23.59% increase in Hg(II) absorption, a phenomenon correlated with the upregulated expression of the mechanosensitive channel Piezo1 and the metal transporter DMT1. This integrated organ-on-a-chip system provides a robust tool for studying heavy metal toxicology and understanding the mechanical regulation of intestinal absorption.
humanized_abstract: The absorption mechanism of mercury ions (Hg(II)) in the human intestine has so far been studied by animal experiments and static cell culture. However, these models are far from the true physiological microenvironment of the intestine and are unable to provide real-time dynamic information about the intestinal absorption of heavy metals. Here, we developed an organ-on-a-chip gut model device with label-free sensors to dynamically mimic the physiological environment of the intestine and realize the intestinal absorption of Hg(II) in situ. Our gut-on-a-chip device with the added benefit of function can not only achieve the dynamic intestinal environment by applying the optimized fluid flow and cyclic stretching (0.02 dyne/cm2 and 1%, 0.15 Hz) but also mimic the shape of the intestinal villi and improve the degree of cellular differentiation. The added TEER and electrochemical sensors can dynamically monitor the integrity of the intestinal barrier and Hg(II) transport. Our experiments showed that the intestinal barrier integrity and the intestinal physiological environment simulated by the fluid flow and cyclic stretching can improve the formation of intestinal villi and promote the degree of cellular differentiation, which were significantly different from those in static cell culture. The exposure of Hg(II) ions to the intestinal barrier will lead to physical destruction of the epithelial barrier, which leads to a significant decrease in TEER and a decrease in the expression of ZO-1 and Ezrin proteins. By changing the tensile strain from 1% to 5%, the intestinal absorption of Hg(II) increased by 23.59% and was significantly correlated with the upregulation of Piezo1 and DMT1.
